text: EXPERIMENTAL MOLTEN-SALT-FUELED 30-Mw POWER REACTOR
EXPERIMENTAL MOLTEN-SALT-FUELED 30-Mw POWER REACTOR
The design plant electrical output is 10,000 kw, and the total construction cost is estimated to be approximately $16,000,000.

With this objective in mind, a single-region homogeneous burner employing a semidirect-maintenance concept, operating on a Loeffler steam cycle, and producing 10 Mw of electricity was chosen as a reference design.
It has been estimated that this experimental reactor would cost approximately $16,000,000 and that about two and one-half years would be required for construction.
The general characteristics of the system are listed in Table 1.

section: GENERAL DESCRIPTION OF REACTOR AND PLANT LAYOUT
text: As previously mentioned, the induced radioactivity in the coolant salt would decay rapidly, and direct maintenance could be performed after a few minutes' delay to allow the 11-sec fluorine activity to decay.
The fuel will be added as solid $\mathsf{U}^{235}\mathsf{F}_4$ at a free liquid surface in the fuel system expansion tank or in a separate vessel in which a free surface is presented.

It is estimated that $70\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ would have to be added to the system during the first two years of power operation (30 Mw at $100\%$ plant factor) to take care of burnup and fission product override.
This quantity is equivalent to a daily addition of 96 g of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ .
Considering the density of $\mathsf{UF}_4$ to be $6.70\mathrm{g/cm^3}$ , it may be seen that approximately $14.3\mathrm{cm^3}$ of $\mathsf{UF}_4$ would have to be added daily.
The quantity of fissionable material, $\Delta M$ , consumed per day was calculated, as mentioned above, to be $96\mathrm{g}$ .

For a temperature coefficient of $-6.75 \times 10^{-5}$ , an assumed mass reactivity coefficient of 9, and a critical inventory of 90 kg, a mean temperature drop of less than $2^{\circ}F$ per day would be experienced.

Several experiments have been run in which slugs of $\mathsf{UF}_4$ weighing approximately $40\mathrm{g}$ have been inserted into $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ LiF-BeF $_2$ salt to study solution rates and homogeneity of the $\mathsf{UF}_4$ in the melt.
A fuel fill and drain system having a storage capacity of approximately 400 ft³ is provided.
This system is made up of four vertical cylinders 2.5 ft in diameter and 20 ft high.

It was assumed that the fuel temperature rise would be limited to $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and that a heat sink of 300 kw would be adequate to maintain the temperature below this maximum, even with an excursion of $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ resulting from fuel flow stoppage.
One hundred kilograms of $U^{235}$ was considered to be contained in a single vessel and
Running time, one year; volume of salt, $175\mathrm{ft}^3$ ; heat capacity of salt, 64 Btu·ft $^{-3}$ ·(°F) $^{-1}$ .
When the $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ was contained in the lower half of the vessel, a 10-ft-high by 2.5-ft-dia volume, the multiplication constant was 0.596.
When the $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ was considered to be contained in the bottom as a cube 2.5 ft on a side, the multiplication constant increased to 0.827.
Estimates have been made of the shielding required for carrier flasks containing $2\text{ft}^3$ of fuel.
This calculation was made on the basis of one year of operation exposure at full power with 2.32 days of cooldown and no self-shielding in the fuel.
11, is connected to a thermally insulated stainless steel jacket which surrounds the entire reactor assembly.

A centrifugal blower capable of delivering 3000 cfm with a pressure differential of 5 in. of water at a gas temperature of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ recirculates gas through the heater-cooler package and then around the reactor assembly.
One hundred eighty kilowatts of heater capacity is designed into the heater sections in the form of high-temperature $(1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ alloy sheath tubular elements.
A water-cooled fin-and-tube heat exchanger capable of handling $250\mathrm{kw}$ is used as a heat dump.
A preliminary estimate indicates that a cold reactor system can be preheated to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in less than two days by using forced air circulation.
As previously mentioned, a portion (approximately $10\%$ ) of the fuel flow is bypassed through a gas stripping system.
Helium is admitted into the expansion tank from a fuel pump purge at a rate of 1 scfh.

text: These vessels have been sized to give a maximum liberation rate of approximately 10 cfm per square foot of water surface.

text: Steam at 1450 psia and $1000^{\circ}F$ is supplied to a 3600-rpm condensing turbine.

section: STEAM GENERATING EQUIPMENT AND TURBINE-ELECTRIC SYSTEM
text: With a net efficiency of $33.3\%$ , the net electrical power output of the plant is 10 Mw.
1) were modified slightly for the purpose of evaluating the nuclear performance.
The fuel is considered to be contained in a quasi-spherical INOR-8 vessel having a diameter of 72 in. and a wall $\frac{1}{2}$ in. thick.

The core is surrounded by a neutron and gamma-ray "thermal" shield consisting of the following annular regions: a gas annulus 3 in. thick through which dry air or nitrogen may be circulated to preheat the shell during startup or to cool it during operation; a 6-in. layer of aluminum silicate insulation (6 lb/ft³); a 2-in. layer of ordinary steel; a 4-in. layer of organic coolant (diphenyl or related compounds); and four successive 2-in. layers of boron steel (1% B) separated by four 4-in. layers of organic coolant.
Draining the organic coolant from the first layer (adjacent to the ordinary steel) will result in a reactivity decrease (1.89%) expected to be sufficient for emergency shutdown.
The fuel carrier consists of a mixture of LiF (63 mole %) and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ (37 mole %) having a liquidus temperature of $860^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and an estimated density of $1.9\mathrm{g/cm}^3$ .

The critical concentration of $\mathrm{U}^{235}$ (as $\mathrm{UF}_4$ ) is estimated to be $3.25 \times 10^{19}$ atoms/cm³ (0.1 mole %), giving a critical mass of $40.6\mathrm{kg}$ and a critical inventory of $65.8\mathrm{kg}$ for a total fuel volume of $171\mathrm{ft}^3$ .

At a power level of 30 Mw(th), the fission rate adjacent to the reactor vessel is approximately $7.5 \times 10^{10}$ fissions·cm $^{-3}$ ·sec $^{-1}$ , and the power density is approximately $2.4 \, \text{W/cm}^3$ .
It may be seen that $55\%$ of the fissions are epithermal.
Inventories and neutron balances for the initial state and after operation for two and five years at 30 Mw(th) are given in Table 4.

In the initial state, the neutron leakage from the spherical thermal shield amounts to about $10^{8}$ neutrons $\cdot \mathrm{cm}^{-2} \cdot \mathrm{sec}^{-1}$ at a power level of 30 Mw(th).
Spectral Distribution of Neutrons Leaking from Thermal Shield of 30-Mw Experimental Molten-Salt-Fueled Reactor.

The gamma-ray heating in the core vessel was estimated by means of the Oracle code GHIMSR to be $2.5\mathrm{w/cm}^3$ and the strength of the gamma-ray current at the surface of the core vessel was estimated to be $5\mathrm{w/cm}^2$ .
It was estimated that the photon current leaking from the thermal shield would not exceed $0.001\mathrm{w/cm}^2$ .

Exploratory studies were made of a generally similar reactor of 600-Mw(th) capacity,4 and it was tentatively concluded that the reactor
Spectral Distribution of Gamma Energy Leaking from Thermal Shield of 30-Mw Experimental Molten-Salt-Fueled Reactor.
In order to compensate for the partial loss of delayed neutrons arising from the precursor deficiency, the reactor is just critical when $k$ exceeds 1 by the appropriate amount.

When circulation is suddenly stopped, the precursor concentration gradually rises to the steady-state value appropriate to a stationary-fuel reactor (i.e., $k = 1$ ), and the corresponding increase in delayed-neutron production tends to make the reactor supercritical.

The temperature rises about $35^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in the first 10 sec, and the total rise in temperature is less than $110^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ; $90\%$ of the maximum is reached 90 sec after the start of the transient.
The initial rate of rise, which is important for thermal shock considerations, is very modest, as shown by the curve giving the rise in the first 10-sec period.

Typically, for 3 Mw heat removal, the first maximum occurs in 82 sec following initiation of the transient, with a $55^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ peak (above the new critical temperature); the second maximum occurs 9 min after
initiation of the transient, with a $5^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ peak.
The finished INOR-8 components in the plant were estimated to weigh approximately 50,000 lb.
Facilities for supplying INOR-8 stock are available, and a sizable inventory is currently on hand.
The gross volume of the reactor building, offices, hot cells, laboratories, and control room was estimated at 200,000 ft³.
The turbine-generator building was calculated to have a volume of 400,000 ft³.
This quantity included a spare fuel volume and $50\%$ overage for the coolant-salt volume, resulting in a total of 550 ft³.
This entire quantity was assumed to contain Li⁷ at the $99.99\%$ assay level, which contributes one-third of the total cost of $1500 per cubic foot.
After the completion of shakedown and planned experiments, it was concluded that the plant could be operated for $635,000 a year.

A heat baffle 51 in. long was added at the outlet end of the steam annulus to insulate the steam from the water so that $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ superheat could be obtained.
